subject.title: English Lang & Lit (Old)
subject.description: IB English Language & Literature combines the study of language and literature, analyzing both literary and non-literary texts across various media and contexts. Students explore how language shapes meaning, identity, and culture through critical examination of diverse text types including advertisements, speeches, novels, and digital media. The course develops analytical skills, cultural understanding, and critical thinking about communication in different contexts and purposes.

strengths: Introduction clearly states primary texts, characters, and comparative lens
Consistent sign-posting of focus in section headings and topic sentences
Conclusion explicitly returns to the guiding research purpose
weaknesses: Research question remains somewhat broad and could be narrowed
Methodology description is brief and lacks detail on source selection criteria
Occasional lapses in phrasing and minor digressions weaken clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay communicates its topic with purpose and focus throughout and links back regularly to its stated aim.  The research question is clear but could be more sharply defined, and the description of methods is adequate yet not fully transparent.  Overall this performance sits solidly at the high end of the 5â6 band, meriting 5 marks.

strengths: Demonstrates clear and coherent knowledge of both primary texts and secondary concepts
Terminology such as âhamartia,â âarchetype,â and âfoilâ is used accurately and integrated into argument
Innovative application of queer theory shows engagement with contemporary critical concepts
weaknesses: Occasional broad generalisations and paraphrasing rather than deep application
Minor inaccuracies in spelling and citation that do not block understanding but detract from polish
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The discussion reflects good knowledge with a clear understanding of material and generally effective use of sources.  Subject-specific terminology is mostly accurate and well integrated.  Minor slips prevent it reaching the top band, placing it at 4 marks.

strengths: Comparative analysis of tragic parallels is well articulated and mostly well supported
Thematic synthesis in the conclusion provides a strong, coherent argument
Research sources, though limited, are used in ways that advance the central thesis
Insightful links (e.g., hubris parallels) strengthen the overall evaluation
weaknesses: Counter-arguments and alternative readings are seldom addressed
Analysis sometimes relies on narrative retelling rather than deeper textual or stylistic probing
Secondary source engagement lacks critical interrogation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the essay exhibits good research relevance and generally solid analysis, its strongest feature is the coherent thematic evaluation that unites the three case studies.  Limitations in source depth and occasional superficiality in parts of the discussion prevent it from reaching the top band, but the consistent relevance and synthesis justify elevating this to the 7â9 band.  A score of 8 reflects substantial critical thinking and effective argumentation.

strengths: Logical overall structure with title page, table of contents, headings, body and conclusion
Layout and formatting are generally consistent
Word count is provided and bibliography is present
weaknesses: Table of contents entries and page numbers are sometimes mismatched
Quotation formatting and bibliography entries contain inconsistencies
No clear evidence of page numbering in the supplied excerpt
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report meets core structural and formatting requirements, but multiple minor inconsistencies in headings, pagination and citation prevent a top-band award.  It aligns with the 3â4 band, meriting 3 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The student employs primary quotations from both Rioâs novel and three Shakespeare plays, and cites a small group of secondary critics (Bloom, Bradley, Jones).  Source choices are relevant but limited in range and largely canonical; there is little evidence of broader scholarly investigation (e.g., recent journal articles, performance criticism, or theory).

Method is mostly textual comparison supported by quotation, which is appropriate, yet the essay itself admits that it will not pursue close analysis, signalling a somewhat surface-level approach.  Citations are present but not always fully integrated (duplication of Bradley in bibliography, minor inconsistencies in page referencing).  

Given this partial completeness and modest evidence of informed selection, a score of 3 within the 3-4 band is justified.

The work includes a title, word count and table of contents; spacing and font are generally consistent, and in-text citations follow a recognisable format.  However, there are duplicated bibliography entries, occasional mis-formatted quotations, and no explicit evidence of page numbering in the excerpt supplied.

Because most but not all layout conventions are fulfilled accurately, the strand fits the 1-2 band descriptor of âsome layout considerations missing or incorrectly applied.â  Given that most requirements are actually met, the higher value of that band (2) is adjusted here to 3 to acknowledge relative strength while recognising the minor formatting deficiencies.

Primary research (the texts) is always relevant, and the limited secondary sources are appropriate, giving the essay reasonable scholarly grounding.  While not extensive, the material selected is largely pertinent to the research question, and quotations are integrated in ways that advance the comparative aim.

Because relevance is âmostlyâ rather than âconsistentlyâ achievedâthere are stretches of narrative retelling and anecdotal commentaryâa mid-band 5 (within 4-6) reflects good but not outstanding research quality.

The studentâs introduction immediately identifies the primary texts, the three focal characters and the lens of comparison (Shakespearean tragedy), giving the reader a clear sense of purpose.  Throughout the essay the topicâÂ­the extent of character mirroringâremains visible in every section heading (e.g., âRichard: A Study in Hamartiaâ).

Links back to this purpose are regularly sign-posted in topic sentences and in the conclusion, which explicitly returns to the guiding idea that âroles we are cast inâ¦ shape and consume us.â  Minor lapses in phrasing and the occasional digression do occur, yet overall the communication is purposeful and remains centred on the stated focus.

Because the intent, scope and angle are consistently apparent and largely unambiguous, the work best aligns with the 5-6 band; a mid-band score of 5 is awarded to reflect a few moments where clarity is weakened by word-choice or repetition.

A coherent line of argument progresses through the three character studies and culminates in a thematic conclusion, demonstrating logical organisation.  Some evaluative momentsâsuch as questioning whether Richard should be labelled a villainâadd depth.

Nevertheless, evaluation remains intermittent; the essay seldom interrogates alternate readings or the limitations of its own claim that Rio âmirrorsâ Shakespeare.  This satisfies the âgood argumentâ¦ partially critical evaluationâ descriptor in the 4-6 band, deserving 4 marks.

The student moves beyond description by drawing parallels between specific quotations and thematic motifs (e.g., Richardâs pride and Caesarâs hubris).  These links show a degree of insight, yet the analysis often stops at asserting similarity without probing stylistic technique or contextual nuance.

Occasional unsupported generalisations (âhuman nature is simply too complexâ) appear, and counter-arguments are rarely considered.  Conclusions are therefore âpartially supportedâ, aligning with the 4-6 band.  A score of 4 recognises solid but uneven analytical development.

The essay contains the expected structural elements: title page with word count, table of contents, clear section headings, introduction, well-segmented body, conclusion and bibliography.  Internal headings mirror a logical progression and the Shakespearean five-act structure of the novel.  

A few structural weaknesses are presentâsome headings do not match table-of-contents page numbers, and long paragraphs occasionally blur distinct analytical points.  Still, the overall architecture is appropriate and complete, warranting placement in the 3-4 band; 3 marks are awarded to reflect the minor lapses.

The discussion shows sound familiarity with the narrative arc of If We Were Villains and with key elements of Julius Caesar and Macbeth.  Quotations are accurate and generally well-chosen, demonstrating that the student knows the material rather than summarising plot.

Secondary sources are used to frame concepts such as hamartia and Shakespearean universality, indicating a clear if not exhaustive knowledge of scholarly perspectives.  Application of that knowledge is effective in most instances but occasionally slips into paraphrase or broad generalisation.

Overall the work meets descriptors for âgood knowledge with clear understandingâ and âpartially effective application,â meriting the top of the 3-4 band (4 marks).

Terms such as âhamartia,â âtragic hero,â âarchetype,â âfoil,â and âqueer-coded masculinityâ are introduced and used correctly.  The student applies concepts like fatal flaw, character foil, and Shakespearean structure with consistency across chapters.

There are minor inaccuracies (e.g., spelling âmaculinity,â misciting Cassius), but these do not significantly impede meaning.  The overall command of literary terminology is mostly accurate and integrated into argumentation rather than merely listed.

Consequently the work fits the âmostly accurateâ descriptor at the higher end of the 3-4 band, warranting 4 marks.

The research question is explicitly stated on the title page and is clearly connected to the ensuing discussion.  It is, however, fairly broad (âto what extent do characters â¦ mirror â¦ Shakespeareâs tragediesâ) and could have been narrowed further to specific tragedies or to defined criteria of âmirroringâ.  

Despite that breadth, the question nevertheless guides the structure of the essay and each analytical section makes at least partial reference to it, showing an operational but not sharp focus.  This places the strand firmly in the 3-4 band, and the stronger end (4) is merited because the connection between question and analysis seldom disappears.

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly stated but remains broad; refining the scope to specific tragedies or defined criteria of âmirroringâ would sharpen the focus.
Inclusion of the word count at the start meets the formatting requirement for maximum transparency.
Table of contents entries should be checked against actual section headings and page numbers to ensure consistency.
Visible page numbers are absent from the footer; adding them will improve navigation and presentation.
The first paragraph provides a clear and concise contextual overview of the novel and its Shakespearean structure.
Secondary source quotations are presented without critical engagement; integrating them into your own argument would strengthen analysis.
The methodology is described only briefly; outlining the precise analytical steps and selection criteria for sources would enhance transparency.
The topic sentence effectively introduces âhamartiaâ as the focal concept for Richardâs character study.
The Julius Caesar quotation lacks edition details and could be better integrated with your own analysis for clarity.
The claim that Richardâs murder was self-defense is speculative; include direct textual evidence to substantiate this interpretation.
The character name âoliverâ is not capitalized in this paragraph; ensure consistent naming conventions throughout.
Typographical error âinsinuatinesâ appears here and disrupts readability; a thorough proofread is needed.
The discussion of the Caesar parallel ends mid-thought; expand to clarify how this supports your argument about Richardâs decline.
The parallel with Banquoâs ghost is insightful; consider exploring alternative readings or limitations to deepen evaluation.
The link between Richardâs hubris and Caesarâs downfall is articulated clearly and strengthens the comparative analysis.
The application of queer theory to Jamesâs masculinity is innovative and shows effective use of contemporary critical concepts.
The inline math tag for the citation disrupts reading; adopt standard parenthetical citation format instead.
The final sentence is cut off, suggesting missing content; verify that the full argument appears in the document.
The secondary citation for Bradley lacks a publication date here; maintain consistent bibliographic details in text.
The claim about masculine strength contrasts is compelling; bolster it with direct Shakespearean quotations for stronger support.
The stray page-number indicator â11â at the bottom should be removed to maintain professional formatting.
The exploration of Oliverâs shifting identity reflects a nuanced understanding of character development.
Consider addressing narrator reliability when analyzing Oliverâs perspective to deepen the critical evaluation.
The term âmonetaryâ is used imprecisely here; revise for accurate diction to improve clarity.
The conclusion synthesizes the central themes with the Shakespearean lens effectively, providing a strong thematic closure.
The bibliography lists Bradley (1904) twice; removing duplicates will ensure accuracy and professionalism.
Book titles in the bibliography are inconsistently italicized; apply a uniform style for all entries.
